title: Emergency Guide: Cat Convulsing and Foaming at the Mouth
meta_desc: If your cat is convulsing and foaming at the mouth, this is a high-priority emergency. Learn what to do immediately and how to seek urgent vet care.
content_html:
What is happening to my cat?

When a cat experiences convulsions, it is often undergoing a seizure, which is a sudden surge of electrical activity in the brain. This can cause involuntary muscle contractions, loss of consciousness, and paddling of the limbs. The 'foaming at the mouth' is typically hypersalivation (excessive drooling) caused by the cat's inability to swallow during the episode or the rapid movement of the jaw churning saliva into a froth.

Is this an emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is a critical medical emergency. While a single, short seizure may not be immediately fatal, it is often a symptom of an underlying life-threatening condition such as toxin ingestion (like lilies or antifreeze), head trauma, organ failure, or neurological disorders. Continuous or repeated seizures (status epilepticus) can lead to brain damage or fatal hyperthermia.

Why a photo or video helps triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the clinic, capturing a quick video of the event can be incredibly helpful for your veterinarian. Seizures can look very similar to other conditions like fainting (syncope) or severe tremors. Seeing the specific physical movements and the cat's level of consciousness helps the vet determine the type of neurological event and select the most effective emergency treatment immediately upon your arrival.

faqs.question: What should I do while my cat is convulsing?
faqs.answer: Keep your hands away from the cat's mouth to avoid accidental bites. Move furniture away to prevent injury, turn off bright lights, and keep the environment quiet. Do not attempt to restrain the cat.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat any medication at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human or leftover pet medications during a seizure, as this can worsen the condition or cause choking. Only a veterinarian should administer anti-convulsants.

faqs.question: How long do these episodes usually last?
faqs.answer: A typical seizure lasts between 30 seconds to 2 minutes. If a seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes, it is a life-threatening crisis requiring immediate intervention to stop the cycle.
